title: Theory of Rayleigh molecular light scattering by isotropic polar fluids revisited
abstract: The molecular theory of Rayleigh light scattering in dense isotropic polar fluids is reconsidered by suitably adapting local field concepts of electrostatics to propagating electromagnetic waves, hence accounting for both the rotational and dipole-induced dipole (DID) contributions. Simple analytical equations are derived for the various Rayleigh ratios relevant to lateral light scattering in various situations, namely pure DID, pure rotations and mixed contributions. For pure DID, the derived Rayleigh ratios are entirely analytical and very simple, while for pure rotation, the use of rotational mean field approximation is justified, hence allowing the description of Rayleigh ratios in terms of a single orientational correlation parameter that is straightforwardly determined as a positive root of a quadratic algebraic equation. Simple expressions for the Rayleigh ratios are also derived in two mixed situations where DID dominates rotation and when rotation dominates DID. Relation to previous experimental and theoretical work is discussed.
summary: This paper revisits the theory of Rayleigh light scattering in dense polar liquids by integrating molecular rotation and dipole-induced dipole effects into a unified analytical framework. It provides simple, parameter-free equations that accurately predict scattering intensities and depolarization ratios across different liquid types. The work resolves long-standing discrepancies in how light scattering scales with the internal local field.
main_problem: The lack of correct analytical formulas for Rayleigh light scattering ratios in dense isotropic polar fluids that account for both molecular rotation and the dipole-induced dipole (DID) mechanism.
main_result: The derivation of simple analytical equations for Rayleigh ratios that successfully describe both rotation-dominated and DID-dominated regimes, showing excellent agreement with experimental data.
method: A classical statistical mechanical approach using radiation theory and local field concepts within the first Born approximation, supplemented by a rotational mean field approximation.
model_or_system: Dense isotropic polar fluids (such as benzene, toluene, and carbon tetrachloride) modeled as symmetric tops with molecular polarizability anisotropy.
key_observables: Rayleigh ratios (isotropic R_iso, vertical R_VV, horizontal R_HH, and depolarized R_VH), vertical depolarization ratio (r_v), and the derivative of the squared refractive index.
important_parameters: Molecular polarizability anisotropy (kappa), orientational correlation parameters (g_V^H, G), internal field factor (L), and the DID-induced anisotropy (kappa_DID).
main_assumptions: Use of the first Born approximation, neglect of three-body contributions, and the application of the rotational mean field approximation.
figures_summary: The notes do not provide a specific description of figures, though they mention tables comparing theoretical predictions to experimental data for various liquids.
paper_structure: The paper identifies theoretical inconsistencies in existing models, develops a new framework based on local field theory and radiation theory, derives analytical solutions for different scattering regimes, and validates the theory against experimental data for several organic liquids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
